explanation_md: The first loop uses `int x` — a copy. Multiplying `x` by 10 changes only the copy, not the element in the vector. The second loop prints the original values 1, 2, 3 unchanged.
The first loop uses `int& x` — a reference to each element. Multiplying `x` by 10 modifies the actual vector elements. The second loop prints the modified values: 10, 20, 30.

prompt_md: When should you prefer `const int& x` over plain `int x` as the range variable in a for-each loop?

options.a: Whenever you want to modify the elements in place
options.b: When the element type is large or non-trivially copyable, to avoid copying each element
options.c: When you need to access the element's index inside the loop body
options.d: Only when the vector is declared `const`

explanation_md: `const int& x` binds a read-only reference to each element, avoiding a copy. For small types like `int` the difference is negligible, but for larger types it saves copying the entire object on each iteration. Option A describes `int& x` (non-const reference). Option C — the index — is not available in range-based loops at all.

options.a: The range-based for loop cannot be used to modify vector elements at all
options.b: `x` is a copy of each element; modifying it does not affect the vector
options.c: The loop must use `auto` instead of `int`
options.d: The loop iterates in the wrong direction

explanation_md: When you write `int x : v`, `x` receives a copy of each element. Changes to `x` are invisible to the vector. To modify the actual elements, declare the variable as a reference: `int& x : v`.

prompt_md: Which loop is the most appropriate for this task: given `std::vector<int> grades`, count how many elements are above 90?

options.a: An index-based loop, because you need to track positions
options.b: A range-based for loop with `int grade : grades`, since you only need the values
options.c: A range-based for loop with `int& grade : grades`, because you might modify the grades later
options.d: A reverse index-based loop, to avoid the signed/unsigned issue

explanation_md: You only need to read each value — no index, no modification. A range-based for loop with a value copy (`int grade`) is the clearest choice. Option C adds `&` unnecessarily. Option A and D add complexity with no benefit for this task.
